content:
Cases
* While the ordering itself isn't critical for this clash, it's still preferable to group similar cases together. Currently (T = Test, V = Validator):

  - w < h: T2, T3, T4, V6
  - w = h: T1, T5, T6
  - w > h: V1, V2, V3, V4, V5

  Changes to make:
  - Group the w < h cases into two pairs
  - Group the w > h cases (excluding V5) into two pairs
  - Group the w = h cases (excluding T5) into one pair
  - Keep the value of g similar in each pair, including one pair where g = 0, and assign distinct ranges of g to the remaining pairs (e.g. 1 to 3, 4 to 6, and so on).

* Consider adding a pair of cases for each of the scenario below (i.e. 3 pairs in total):

  - w = 1 and h > 1.

  - h = 1 and w > 1.

  - w = h = 1 with different values of g.

Thanks.

What is [[w]] and [[h]] meant to represent?
It looks like [[w]] is the number of vertical separators ({{|}} and {{+}} on intersections), while [[h]] is the number of whitespaces, sections created by the horizontal separators.
From the example:
`o | | | o` - four spaces, three vertical separators; [[w]] is 3
```
 o
 
-
 
-
 
o
```
- three spaces, two horizontal separators; [[h]] is also 3

And while it would be quite simple to deduce this from test cases, did you indent to include this inconsistency?

Also, it seems that this CoC is only for Reverse mode at the moment. Do you plan to make it Fastest/Shortest as well?

Hi @AdSm!

Sorry about the confusing clash and inconsistencies. I had a really wild day yesterday, so I messed up a lot. Anyways, I've given up on making this into a fastest/shortest clash and will just make this Reverse only. Additionally, the width [[w]] and height [[h]] should make more sense now. Could you take a look at this puzzle again and tell me what you think? Thank you!

Hi @5DN1L!

Not sure if I did what you asked correctly, but here are the changes I made to the cases: (T = Test, V = Validator)
T1/V1: w < h
T2/V2: w > h
T3/V3: w = h
T4/V4: g is a really large value and w = h in those cases
T5/V5: g = 1 and w = h in those cases
T6/V6: g = 0 and w > h in one of them and w < h in the other
T7/V7: w = h = 1
T8/V8: h = 1 and w > 1
T9/V9: w = 1 and h > 1
T10/V10: really large tennis rackets

One more thing; today, I just tried out my clash in the IDE, and sometimes, this appeared instead of the tennis racket pattern: ?GREEN?Success?GREEN?

Is this normal and okay for clashes like this, or could this become a problem for other languages (I still got 100% in Python)
Would you share a screenshot somewhere or send it to me via private message on the forum?

Getting the screenshot to you is a bit tricky for me currently, but meanwhile, I think you could paste my solution into the IDE, run it, and you might be able to see the same thing.

import sys
import math

# Initialization
w, h, g = map(int, input().split())

# Racket Grid
print("o"*(w*2+1))
for y in range(h):
  print(end="o ")
  for x in range(w-1):
    print(end="| ")
  print(end="o")
  print()
  if y!=h-1:
    print(end="o-")
    for x in range(w-1):
      print(end="+-")
    print(end="o")
    print()
print("o"*(w*2+1))

# Grip
for i in range(g):
  if i==g-1:
    print(" "*(w-1)+"|_|")
  else:
    print(" "*(w-1)+"| |")

Yeah, now it is consistent and easier to understand. And as you've made it reverse only, you don't have to worry about explaining the racket grid.

Maybe I would change the first test to have all values distinct, to allow players to take only a quick look (once they know the pattern) at it and know which value corresponds to what. But they'd know that from the second test anyway, so it doesn't really matter that much.

Thanks for the feedback @AdSm! Glad to know that it's better for you now.

Also, I changed the value of [[g]] for Test 1 so that the values are distinct. Hopefully that helps :)
